title: Velvet Casino, Vol. 1
tonic_progression: Cm (Dorian, the threshold — stepping in from the rain) -> Gm (Dorian, the floor opens, the velvet room) -> Dm (Aeolian, the table found, the first quiet bets) -> Am (Dorian, the cards warm, the night settles) -> Em (Dorian, the luck begins to rise) -> Bm (Dorian, the streak builds, chips stacking) -> D major (Mixolydian-warm relative lift, THE HOT STREAK PEAK — luck at its sweetest) -> Bm (Aeolian, the warmth tips, the first cold card) -> F#m (Aeolian, the turn — the house leans in) -> C#m (Phrygian color, the squeeze, the table goes quiet) -> G#m / Abm (Aeolian, the house collects, the chips are gone) -> Cm (Aeolian, the ring closes — out the door into the rain, the same tonic from the far side of the night)
narrative_arc: Twelve tracks read as a single slow night at a rain-slicked midnight casino, from the door to the gutter and out again — a luck-rises-then-the-house-wins arc told in slow, sultry noir-synthwave with lounge saxophone and brushed drum machines. Tracks 1-2 are the arrival: a lone figure steps in from the rain through a flickering gold-and-crimson marquee, cigarette smoke hanging in the lamplight (1, C minor Dorian, the only drumless track, the velvet pad and a single far-off smoke-sax phrase establishing the album's smoky tonal home), and the floor opens — brushed drum machine ticking in, the SMOKE-SAX THEME nosing in low over a velvet-room groove as the room reveals its red drapes and gold rails (2, G minor Dorian, the sultry pulse locks in). Tracks 3-6 are the slow build of luck: the table is found and the first quiet bets are placed, the ROULETTE FIGURE introduced as a slow spinning arpeggio (3, D minor, the table found), the cards warm and the night settles into an easy groove (4, A minor Dorian), the luck begins to rise as the smoke-sax lead turns confident and the harmony warms toward the major (5, E minor Dorian, the luck rising), and the streak builds with chips stacking, the groove firming, the roulette figure spinning faster and surer (6, B minor Dorian, the build). Track 7 is THE HOT STREAK PEAK — the album's brightest moment and warmest harmony, the only major key, the lone figure on an unbeatable run, the smoke-sax soaring resolved over a warm Mixolydian groove, luck at its absolute sweetest (7, D major, the hero moment, BPM ceiling 104 — sultry-warm exhilaration, never frantic). Tracks 8-11 are the turn and the house winning: the warmth tips and the first cold card falls, the major slipping back to minor as a faint unease enters (8, B minor, the turn begins), the house leans in and the odds quietly reverse, the smoke-sax going wary (9, F# minor, the turn), the squeeze — the table goes quiet and tight, Phrygian menace creeping under the velvet as the chips bleed away (10, C# minor Phrygian color, the squeeze), and the house collects, the chips gone, the roulette figure slowing and finally stopping dead on the wrong number (11, G#/Ab minor, the house wins, the album's darkest and most tender point). Track 12 closes the ring: the figure walks out past the still-flickering marquee into the rain with empty pockets, the album's opening velvet-pad and smoke-sax theme played one last time low and unhurried an octave down, the night's gamble fully spent, the neon reflected in the wet street (12, C minor, the ring closed at the door, the listener arrives at the same tonic from the far side of a long, losing night). The house always wins, but the night was velvet while it lasted.

recurring_motifs.motif: SMOKE-SAX THEME — a long-breathed, slow, sultry lounge-saxophone melodic line (voiced on a breathy sax-flavored analog lead or a real-sax-style sample bed: slow portamento between phrase peaks, a smoky filter-envelope breath on each entry, lots of late-night reverb tail), the album's noir-casino signature and emotional throughline. It is the voice of the lone figure at the wheel — confident on the rise, soaring at the streak, wary at the turn, and exhausted-but-still-breathing at the loss. Slow and low and smoky throughout; never fast or shrill (this is lounge noir, not a chase).
recurring_motifs.transformation: Track 1 introduces it FAR-OFF AND ALONE — a single distant smoke-sax phrase drifting through the lamplight over the velvet pad, no rhythm, the album's tonal/emotional thesis stated at the threshold before the night begins. Track 5 plays it CONFIDENT — the sax turning sure and warm over the rising-luck groove as the streak starts, the figure beginning to win. Track 7 (the hot streak peak) plays it SOARING AND RESOLVED — the same contour but now in D major, the breathy tension blooming into exhilaration, the sax finally allowed to ride high and warm over the Mixolydian groove at luck's sweetest (the lounge lead's hero statement). Track 9 (the turn) plays it WARY — slow, low, the portamento dragged and the filter half-closed, the confidence gone, the first doubt in the breath as the house leans in. Track 12 (the close) plays it ALONE AND SPENT — slow, low, unaccompanied an octave down over the Cm velvet pad, the portamento exhausted and the filter rolled off, the night's last word out in the rain, the smoke-sax tired but still breathing.

recurring_motifs.motif: ROULETTE FIGURE — a slow circular arpeggio that spins like a roulette wheel: a six-to-eight-note rotating figure on a glassy bell/FM-celeste voice that cycles round and round, sidechained softly so it breathes, with a subtle rotary/chorus wobble like the wheel's rim catching the light. Its TEMPO-WITHIN-THE-FIGURE is the album's luck-meter: it spins faster and surer as the luck rises, and it slows, drags, and finally STOPS as the house wins. The album's visual-rhythm signature for the spinning wheel under flickering signage.
recurring_motifs.transformation: Track 3 introduces it SLOW AND TENTATIVE — a loose spinning bell arpeggio under the first quiet bets, the wheel turning easy, the listener locking into the circular pulse without consciously hearing it. Track 6 plays it FASTER AND SURER — the figure tightened and confident as the streak builds and the chips stack, the wheel spinning hot. Track 7 (the peak) plays it AT ITS BRIGHTEST AND FULLEST — fully blooming and warm in D major, the wheel spinning at the sweetest run, glints of gold light flying off the rim. Track 10 (the squeeze) plays it SLOWING AND DRAGGING — the spin losing momentum, the figure stretched and pulled flat as the odds turn and the chips bleed, the wheel beginning to lose its turn. Track 11 (the house wins) plays it SLOWING TO A DEAD STOP — the rotation decelerating note by note and finally stopping cold on a single low chromatic landing (the ball dropping into the wrong number), the wheel's last turn, the luck spent.

recurring_motifs.motif: VELVET GROOVE — the album-local rhythmic-textural signature: a brushed drum-machine groove (soft brushed/gated snare on the backbeat, a warm muffled kick, a faint chip-click hi-hat shuffle like clay chips being riffled) laid under a deep, slow, plush analog sub-bass walking a smoky root-and-fifth lounge line, with a wide velvet pad over it. It is what makes Velvet Casino feel SLOW, SULTRY, and INTERIOR (a smoke-filled velvet room, not an open coast or a chase) — low and plush and unhurried, the bass and brushes doing the seduction, never driving.
recurring_motifs.transformation: Track 2 introduces the VELVET GROOVE as the floor opens — the brushed snare ticking in, the warm muffled kick and the plush walking sub-bass locking the sultry room-pulse for the first time, the chip-click shuffle faint underneath. Track 4 settles it into its EASIEST, MOST RELAXED form as the cards warm and the night settles, the groove plush and confident and unhurried. Track 7 (the peak) plays it at its WARMEST AND FULLEST — the brushed groove and walking bass at their plushest under the soaring sax and blooming wheel, the velvet room at its most alive. Track 9 (the turn) plays it TIGHTER AND COOLER — the brushes pulled in, the bass walk shortened and wary, the chip-click shuffle thinning as the house leans in and the room cools. Track 11 (the house wins) plays it SPARSE AND HOLLOW — the brushed snare bled out, the kick muffled to almost nothing, the walking bass reduced to a single slow tolling root as the chips are collected and the room empties, the groove drained of its plush warmth.

tracks.composer_notes: OPENER. Sets the tonal home (C minor Dorian) and the album's signature voices — the wide velvet pad and the SMOKE-SAX THEME, introduced FAR-OFF AND ALONE (a single distant phrase, the album's smoky thesis). This is the album's ONLY drumless track (the threshold breath before the night begins) and tied for its slowest at 82. The Dorian raised-6th (A natural) keeps the minor warm and smoky, not mournful — this is sultry late-night seduction, not grief. The VELVET GROOVE and the ROULETTE FIGURE are deliberately ABSENT here (the room hasn't opened yet — only the pad and the lone sax). Do NOT let MusicGen add drums, a hard kick, or speed this up — it is slow, smoky, plush, and patient; the groove arrives next track. Outro leans toward G (the dominant) priming track 2's G minor (G reread as the G-minor tonic as the floor opens). Keep the rain and neon-hum textural and faint, never busy.

album_notes.overall_form: Arrival -> Slow Build of Luck -> The Hot Streak Peak -> The Turn -> The House Wins -> Out Into the Rain over 12 tracks, a SLOW-BURN-LUCK-RISES-THEN-THE-HOUSE-WINS arc with a RING CLOSED at the home tonic. The shape is a single arc that crests once at the major-key hot streak (track 7) and descends from there into the loss — not a symmetric peak-in-the-middle drive but a long, sultry rise to a sweet crest and a slow, tender fall, framed by the C-minor threshold (track 1) and the C-minor door (track 12). Movements: ARRIVAL (tracks 1-2): stepping in from the rain through a flickering gold-and-crimson marquee, the velvet pad and a far-off smoke-sax establishing the smoky tonal home (1, C minor Dorian, slowest-tied, the only drumless track), and the velvet floor opening as the VELVET GROOVE and SMOKE-SAX THEME lock the sultry room-pulse in (2, G minor Dorian). SLOW BUILD OF LUCK (tracks 3-6): the table found and the first quiet bets placed, the ROULETTE FIGURE introduced spinning slow and tentative (3, D minor Aeolian, a touch cautious), the cards warming as the night settles into its easiest groove (4, A minor Dorian), the luck rising as the smoke-sax turns confident and the harmony warms toward the major (5, E minor Dorian), and the streak building with chips stacking and the wheel spinning hot (6, B minor Dorian, the warm bridge to the peak). THE HOT STREAK PEAK (track 7, D major): the album's single major key and only fully bright moment, the smoke-sax soaring resolved over a warm Mixolydian groove, luck at its absolute sweetest, BPM ceiling 104 (warm sultry exhilaration, never frantic). THE TURN & THE HOUSE WINS (tracks 8-11): the warmth tips and the first cold card falls as the major slips back to its relative minor (8, B minor Aeolian), the house leans in and the smoke-sax goes wary as the odds reverse (9, F# minor Aeolian), the squeeze tightens with the album's only Phrygian menace as the chips bleed and the roulette figure drags (10, C# minor Phrygian), and the house collects with the roulette figure slowing to a dead stop on the wrong number, the album's darkest and most tender point (11, G#/Ab minor Aeolian). OUT INTO THE RAIN (track 12, C minor): the ring closes home on the opening tonic from the far side of the night, the smoke-sax theme played alone and spent, the figure walking out past the still-flickering marquee into the rain with empty pockets, a closed resolved cadence. The C-minor threshold of track 1 and the C-minor door of track 12 frame the night; the luck arc rises to the single warm D-major crest at track 7 and falls into the loss across the descent — that one bright streak amid the smoky minor is the album's thesis: the house always wins, but the night was velvet while it lasted.
album_notes.key_strategy: A DESCENDING-CYCLE-OF-FIFTHS (down a fourth per step) progression that drives the whole night, with ONE warm relative-major lift at the hot-streak peak and a return home to close the ring. The album OPENS on C minor (Dorian) at the threshold, then runs the descending cycle down through ever-deeper tonics, with the MODE tracking the luck-arc: warm Dorian (raised-6th) on the rise (the room generous, the luck warming), natural Aeolian at the cautious table and the turn, a momentary major lift at the peak, and a single Phrygian color at the squeeze: C minor Dorian (threshold) -> G minor Dorian (down a 4th, the velvet floor opens) -> D minor Aeolian (down a 4th, the first bets, mode cools a step for caution) -> A minor Dorian (down a 4th, the cards warm, mode warms back) -> E minor Dorian (down a 4th, the luck rises, leaning toward major) -> B minor Dorian (down a 4th, the streak builds, tipped to the edge of major) -> D major Mixolydian (UP a minor third / relative-major LIFT, THE HOT STREAK PEAK — the only major key, the warm crest) -> B minor Aeolian (DOWN a minor third / relative-minor tip, the warmth tips, the first cold card) -> F# minor Aeolian (down a 4th, the house leans in, the descending engine resumes into the loss) -> C# minor Phrygian (down a 4th, the squeeze, the album's only flat-2 menace) -> G#/Ab minor Aeolian (down a 4th, the house wins, the album's deepest darkest tonic) -> C minor Aeolian (down a 4th, the ring closes HOME on the opening tonic). The descending cycle of fifths (C-G-D-A-E-B then the relative-major peak D and its relative minor B, then F#-C#-G#-C) is the harmonic engine of the slow night sliding from the door to the gutter and back out — the same single engine drives both the luck-rise (warm Dorian) and the house-winning (cooling Aeolian into Phrygian), so the turn feels inevitable rather than a separate section. The hot-streak peak's UP-a-minor-third relative-major lift (B minor -> D major) is the album's only modulation out of minor and reads as EARNED because track 6 tipped its harmony right onto D major's chord tones; track 8's matching DOWN-a-minor-third tip (D major -> B minor) inverts it, dropping the warmth straight back. The album CLOSES with a CLOSED, RESOLVED C-minor cadence (standalone, no open handoff). NO Miami-Vice-style bright Lydian peak or pastel-coast release: this album's single bright moment is a warm bluesy Mixolydian lounge crest, sultry not exhilarant-bright, and it is INTERIOR (a hot streak at a wheel), not a coastal hero-drive.
album_notes.tempo_strategy: A slow, sultry lounge band (80-104) — deliberately the slowest, lowest-band end of the catalog (the audience is unwinding at 2am, not raving; this is lounge noir, not a chase). A patient small-incremented rise to a still-restrained warm peak, then a slow tender wind-down into the loss. ARRIVAL 82 (drumless threshold, tied-slowest) -> 86 (the velvet groove arrives, the floor opens). SLOW BUILD OF LUCK 88 -> 92 -> 96 -> 100 (the four-track rise, the largest reach toward the peak still only 100 — the heat is in the warming harmony, the surer roulette figure, and the confident sax, not in tempo). THE HOT STREAK PEAK 104 (track 7, the BPM CEILING — the warm crest, still sultry not frantic; 104 is the slow-album ceiling, the peak's power is WARMTH and the soaring sax, not speed). THE TURN & HOUSE WINS 98 (the largest single drop, -6, as the first cold card comes off the run) -> 94 -> 90 -> 86 (a slow tender descent through the loss). OUT INTO THE RAIN 80 (the closer, the album's FLOOR — the slowest track, two BPM below the 82 opener: the figure leaves slower and quieter than they arrived, the gamble behind them). The band 80-104 is the catalog's most meditative-sultry (well below Miami Vice's 88-114 cruise-and-chase) — every increment is small and patient; the album never races even at the hot streak. All BPMs within the 80-118 validation range (album range 80-104).
album_notes.motif_strategy: Three album-local motifs (no saga leitmotifs — this is a standalone Vol. 1). (1) SMOKE-SAX THEME — the slow sultry lounge-saxophone line that is the album's emotional throughline and the voice of the lone figure at the wheel: introduced FAR-OFF AND ALONE at the threshold (track 1, drumless thesis), CONFIDENT as the luck rises (track 5), SOARING AND RESOLVED in D major at the hot-streak peak (track 7, the hero statement, the breathy tension blooming into warm exhilaration), WARY as the house leans in and the run turns (track 9, slow/low/dragged-portamento/half-closed-filter), and ALONE AND SPENT an octave down at the close (track 12, exhausted but still breathing, the night's last word in the rain). Its emotional arc — alone, confident, soaring, wary, spent — IS the album's narrative made melodic. Always slow, low, smoky; never fast or shrill (lounge noir, not a chase). (2) ROULETTE FIGURE — a slow circular bell/FM-celeste arpeggio that spins like a roulette wheel, whose TEMPO-WITHIN-THE-FIGURE is the album's luck-meter: introduced SLOW AND TENTATIVE under the first bets (track 3, the wheel just set turning), FASTER AND SURER as the streak builds (track 6, spinning hot), AT ITS BRIGHTEST AND FULLEST in D major at the peak (track 7, the sweetest run, gold light off the rim), SLOWING AND DRAGGING under the squeeze (track 10, losing momentum, stretched flat), and SLOWING TO A DEAD STOP on a single low chromatic landing as the house collects (track 11, the ball dropping into the wrong number, the wheel's last turn). The figure's spin-rate physically tracks the luck — it is the album's clock. (3) VELVET GROOVE — the album-local rhythmic-textural signature (brushed drum-machine groove + plush walking sub-bass lounge line + chip-click hi-hat shuffle + wide velvet pad) that makes the album SLOW, SULTRY, and INTERIOR: introduced as the velvet floor opens (track 2), at its EASIEST AND MOST RELAXED as the cards warm (track 4), at its WARMEST AND FULLEST at the peak (track 7), TIGHTER AND COOLER as the house leans in (track 9), and SPARSE AND HOLLOW as the house collects and the room empties (track 11). Deliberately ABSENT from track 1 (the drumless threshold, before the room opens). The three motifs interlock: the SMOKE-SAX carries the emotion, the ROULETTE FIGURE clocks the luck, and the VELVET GROOVE holds the sultry interior — together they render the whole night without a word.
album_notes.tracklist_continuity: Each track ends on a pitch that primes the next track's tonic — almost entirely via the descending cycle of fifths (each track closes on its own dominant, rerooted as the next track's tonic, the harmonic engine of the slow night sliding from door to gutter and back), with the two relative-third pivots at the peak and the turn. Specifically: 1 ends on G (the dominant of C, rerooted as track 2's G minor — the floor opens). 2 ends on D (V of G, rerooted as track 3's D minor — the table found). 3 ends on A (V of D, rerooted as track 4's A minor — the cards warm). 4 ends on E (V of A, rerooted as track 5's E minor — the luck rises). 5 ends on B (V of E, rerooted as track 6's B minor — the streak builds). 6 ends on F# (V of B, but reread as the major-third leading the harmony UP into track 7's D major — the hot streak crests, the album's one relative-major lift). 7 ends leaning toward B minor with a single faint cool minor color creeping in (the first cold card — D major tipping down a minor third to its relative minor for track 8). 8 ends on F# (V of B, rerooted as track 9's F# minor — the house leans in, the descending engine resumes). 9 ends on C# (V of F#, rerooted as track 10's C# minor — the squeeze). 10 ends on G# (V of C#, rerooted as track 11's G#/Ab minor — the house wins). 11 ends on Eb/D# (V of G#/Ab) BUT turns to LEAVE rather than cadencing dark, resolving home to track 12's C minor (the ring closing). 12 ends on a CLOSED, RESOLVED C-minor cadence (standalone — no open handoff; the night is over, fade to silence). The listener won't consciously hear the descending cycle — they will feel the slow slide from the door down through the loss as inevitable, the one warm D-major crest as luck's real high point, and the return to C minor at the door as the ring closing on a long, losing night.
album_notes.differentiation_from_prior_albums: Velvet Casino is a STANDALONE Vol. 1 (no saga, no shared-universe leitmotifs) and an INTERIOR NOIR-CASINO album — slow, sultry, smoke-filled, gold-and-crimson velvet, a lone figure at a roulette wheel under flickering signage. CRITICAL DISTINCTION FROM THE MIAMI VICE ALBUMS (danger_zone_keys ['miami_vice']): the Miami Vice albums are PASTEL EXTERIOR COAST — a chrome Ferrari Daytona cruise down a turquoise-and-coral neon causeway, palms, ocean, a sax-noir CHASE arc (deal-and-the-run, a propulsive 16th-note chase at 114 BPM, a bright Lydian-major coastal-release peak). Velvet Casino is the OPPOSITE on every axis: INTERIOR not exterior (a velvet casino room, never a coast/causeway/ocean/palm/Daytona/Ferrari), SLOW AND SULTRY not cruise-and-chase (80-104 lounge band vs Miami's 88-114; NO chase track, NO weaponised 16th-note ostinato — the album never races), a JAZZY/LOUNGE harmonic world (Dorian/Aeolian/Phrygian with a warm bluesy Mixolydian crest) not pastel-coast synthwave, and a LUCK-RISES-THEN-THE-HOUSE-WINS gambling arc not a deal-and-the-run pursuit. The sax here is a SLOW LOUNGE smoke-sax (sultry, breathy, low — the voice of a gambler), explicitly NOT Miami's screaming chase-cry sax and NOT a literal Jan-Hammer 'Crockett's Theme' lift. The palette is GOLD-AND-CRIMSON VELVET + cigarette smoke + rain-slicked midnight street, NOT cocaine-pink/turquoise pastel coast. NO imagery overlaps (no palms, ocean, Daytona, causeway, deco-hotel-coast). Versus the rest of the catalog: it shares the descending-cycle-of-fifths engine common to several albums but uses it for a single slow sultry night rather than a saga descent or a propulsive drive; its tempo band (80-104) is the catalog's slowest/most-meditative; and it is purpose-built as lounge noir for 2am — Mike Oldfield's long-form patience + Brian Eno's smoky ambience + a slow-burn lounge-noir crossed with FM-84/The Midnight's melodic warmth held low and sultry, the warm hot-streak crest amid the smoky minor the album's single bright moment. The house always wins, but the night was velvet while it lasted.
album_notes.execution_notes_for_pipeline: MusicGen / ACE-Step is the renderer of record (license risk accepted). Every musicgen_prompt is explicit about 'instrumental' and 'no vocals'; all leads are phrased as instrumental synth/sax voices (no phonemes, no choir 'aahs') — do not relax this in any per-track override. Per-track durations sum to 2940 seconds (49.0 minutes) for the 12 — comfortably over the 45-minute album floor. Long tracks: 6 (270s, the warm bridge/build needs room to crest) and 7 (300s, the hot-streak peak, the album's longest, to give the warm crest its full ride). The rest sit at 240s. All BPMs within the 80-118 validation range (album range 80-104). WEATHER ROUTING — FLAG FOR HUMAN/PIPELINE: the theme is INTERIOR/PLACELESS (a velvet casino on a rain-slicked midnight strip, no fixed real geography), so there is no naturally-themed weather region. As-shipped the album_slug 'velvet-casino-vol-1' has NO branch in nightdrive-encoder::weather::region_for and will FALL THROUGH to a hashed US region. Per the 'TWC cities must match album theme' rule, weather is mandatory on all videos but should match the album's feel: recommend routing this to a US region whose cities read as glamorous late-night gambling/neon-strip cities — i.e. add `if id.contains("velvet") || id.contains("casino") { return &LAS_VEGAS_NEVADA; }` with a Nevada/desert-strip 4-city set (e.g. Las Vegas / Reno / Henderson / Laughlin — all NWS-covered, matching the gold-neon-casino aesthetic), OR accept default US cities by exception if a Vegas region isn't worth adding (the theme is placeless, so default-US is a tolerable fallback here, unlike the explicitly-geographic saga albums). Cover prompts are SDXL-ready (1024x1024, faces hidden, lone-figure-from-behind to avoid realistic-face generation); per the future-albums native-aspect-gen rule, generate covers at native aspect buckets (1024 square + 1344x768 + 768x1344) from scratch rather than outpainting. Per the sync-drop rule for future albums, target a 1-shot synchronized public flip at one anchor timestamp (use run-album --publish-at), NOT the per-track trickle. Mind the YouTube caps for a 12-track album: the ~10/day channel rolling-24h cap, the 6/day GCP per-project video.insert cap, and Pacific-midnight resets — span the upload across a reset or stagger (publish-staggered 6/day) and set the sync-drop anchor accordingly, per the Vol. 2/Vol. 3 lessons. PER THE album_title_danger_zone RULE (reject titles that are BOTH named dialogue/objects in a specific source film AND track titles on that film's canonical soundtrack): all 12 titles were checked against casino-film OSTs (Scorsese's 'Casino' 1995, 'Ocean's Eleven' 2001, James Bond 'Casino Royale' 2006, 'Croupier', '21', 'Rounders') and are generic gambling-DESCRIPTIVE phrases, none matching a specific-film soundtrack track title: 'In From the Rain', 'The Velvet Floor', 'First Bets', 'Easy Money', 'Luck Coming In', 'Stacking Chips', 'House Money', 'The First Cold Card', 'The House Leans In', 'The Squeeze', 'The House Always Wins', 'Out Into the Rain'.
